oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Latest on greenhouse gas emissions and dairy cattle production
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Place, Sara
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: dairy
greenhouse gas
mitigation
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions arise from dairy cattle pro­duction. Estimates for the contribution of dairy to GHG emis­sions vary, but are approximately 1.5% of US GHG emissions. The main sources of GHG emissions from dairy cattle produc­tion are enteric fermentation, manure management, feed pro­duction and on-farm energy use. Mitigation of GHG emissions is possible, with some estimating it’s plausible that U.S. dairy farms could mitigate up to 45% of their emissions per unit of milk produced. However, mitigation should also take a holis­tic approach considering the health and safety implications of strategies, their social acceptance, practicality, the strength of evidence supporting them, and their profitability impacts.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; Vol. 58 No. 2 (2025): 2025 AABP Annual Conference Proceedings; 73-75
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
